title: Enhancing the Efficiency of the Savonius Hydro Turbine in Pipe Through Design Optimization and Computational Fluid Dynamics Flow Modeling
authors: S. Nuyam, R. Suntivarakorn, K. Thanatwutthikorn, P. Vengsungnle
abstract: This work explores the performance improvement of a Savonius Hydro Turbine (SHT) installed inside a 4-inch pipeline by examining the combined effects of the guide-vane angle and central-shaft opening ratio were assessed through Computational Fluid Dynamics simulations supported by experimental measurements. Five turbine configurations (M5-1 to M5-5) were analyzed with the SST-enhanced k–ω turbulence formulation under guide-vane angles of 0°, 25°, 30°, and 35°. The results revealed that both parameters significantly influence the flow field and turbine efficiency. The central-shaft opening reduces reverse flow and torque reversal acting on the retreating blade, whereas the guide vane channels the incoming stream toward the forward-moving blade. Among all configurations, the M5-3 turbine (30 % opening, 35° guide vane) exhibited the most favorable performance, yielding maximum power coefficient (Cp,max) value of 0.3969 (simulation) and 0.3778 (experiment) at Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) close to 0.59. These represent 233 % and 257 % improvements, respectively, compared with the case without a guide vane (M5-3, 0° guide vane). Compared with the solid-shaft turbine (M5-1, 35° guide vane), Cp increased by 48 % (simulation) and 55 % (experiment). The deviation between simulated and experimental Cp ranged from 5.06 % to 12.38 %, decreasing with increasing guide-vane angle, validating the CFD predictions. The combination of a 30 % central-shaft opening and a 35° guide-vane angle produced the most efficient flow interaction, showing strong potential for low-head, in-pipe hydropower recovery in industrial and municipal pipeline systems.

title: Unsteady Vortical Flows and Wake Dynamics of Two-bladed Cycloidal Rotors using Improved Delayed Detached Eddy Simulation at Low Reynolds Number
abstract: Cycloidal rotors are commonly used in aerial and underwater propulsion systems owing to their maneuverability and simple design. However, the complex internal flow dynamics under varying operating conditions remains insufficiently understood. In this study, the three-dimensional flow structures of a two-bladed cycloidal rotor are examined at different advance coefficients (λ) using the Improved Delayed Detached Eddy Simulation (IDDES) model and the sliding mesh technique. The analysis focuses on the force generation regions of both the individual blade and the full rotor, the underlying mechanism of flow-induced performance change, and the vorticity and turbulence characteristics of the wake at low Reynolds numbers. The numerical results are compared with available experimental and computational data, showing a good agreement. Key findings reveal that the force generation regions of the rotor are symmetric, whereas those of the individual blade are asymmetric. Furthermore, the force production area expands when λ<0.31. Variations in λ and rotating speed(n), either individually or together, can significantly alter the stagnation point location, thereby affecting the flow structures on both sides of the blade and, consequently, the performance of the blade and the full rotor. When λ<0.31, the wake exhibits significant deflection, particularly at λ=0.10. Higher rotating speed n results in more energetic wakes with elevated turbulent kinetic energy (TKE), closely associated with three-dimensional vortical structures. An analysis of the three components of the total turbulence production term shows that the streamwise component dominates. Each component is governed by the interaction between the normal Reynolds stresses and the mean velocity gradient, highlighting important considerations for developing effective strategies to reduce wake energy losses.
